title: Contention Shaping
use_for: reducing worker contention and queue hot spots
moving from shared mutation to partitioned ownership
identifying serialization points in a supposedly parallel path
controlling backpressure created by coordination bottlenecks
do_not_use_for: UI architecture as a primary owner
rules: treat contention as a shape-of-ownership problem before a lock-implementation problem
reduce shared write regions before tuning synchronization primitives
make serialization points explicit and measurable
prefer partitioned ownership or staged merge over arbitrary shared mutation
tie backpressure to explicit queue or phase boundaries
procedure: Identify the shared state or queue that bottlenecks workers.
Measure where time is spent waiting, spinning, or serializing.
Rework ownership toward partitioned writes, staged merge, or reduced coordination scope.
Define backpressure behavior if contention can build backlog.
Re-measure throughput, latency, and contention under representative load.

primary_when: the request is specifically about lock contention, queue hot spots, or shared-state coordination bottlenecks
the request is about poor scaling caused by shared mutable ownership or serialization points
must_not_own_when: the main issue is unbounded work, general scheduling policy, or cache locality with no measured contention problem

solves: how to identify and reshape contention bottlenecks
how to turn shared-state coordination into partitioned or staged ownership
how to prevent coordination bottlenecks from dominating runtime latency

core_principles: Contention usually reflects ownership shape before it reflects primitive choice.
A parallel design with one hidden serialization point is still mostly serial.
Backpressure belongs in the design, not as an accidental consequence of a hot queue.
constraints: Contention diagnosis must be tied to real measured waiting or serialization behavior.
Reshaping ownership must preserve correctness before chasing scaling gains.
Backpressure and queue growth should remain observable.
decision_rules: Use contention-shaping as the owner when the central issue is coordination bottlenecks rather than raw dispatch policy.
If workers fight over one shared structure, prefer partitioning or staged reduction before lock micro-tuning.
If queue contention creates visible spikes, attach work-budgeting to keep overflow and backpressure explicit.
If the bottleneck is really false sharing or adjacency, attach cache-optimization after ownership shape is clear.

preferred_patterns: contention metrics around known coordination points
avoid_patterns: many workers writing to one broad shared structure
hidden serialization points inside convenience abstractions
queue buildup with no explicit backpressure policy

recipes.name: Reshape a shared-state bottleneck
recipes.when: Use when worker scaling collapses because too much work funnels through one shared structure.
steps: Measure the contested queue, lock, or shared write region.
Split ownership by partition or stage where possible.
Introduce an explicit merge or reduction step.
Track scaling, latency, and queue buildup after the redesign.

pitfalls: Assuming the right lock primitive will solve broad ownership contention.
Ignoring backpressure while chasing worker throughput.
Treating serialization points as harmless because average throughput still looks acceptable.
review_checklist: Is the contention point explicitly identified and measured?
Was ownership reshaped before synchronization was micro-tuned?
Are backpressure and queue growth visible in the design?
Did the redesign preserve correctness as well as scaling?

references.title: Local Reference: Contention Shaping
references.interpretation: Use this first for shared-state bottlenecks, partitioned ownership, and backpressure-aware contention reduction.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for contention-focused runtime shaping.

references.title: Scheduler
references.interpretation: Use this to keep contention fixes aligned with explicit worker ownership and stage-based dispatch.
references.why: Use to connect contention reshaping with the broader scheduler model.
